title: Heuristic Safety Checks
concept: Pre-filtering proposed actions before they execute to catch format errors or illegal arguments.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we traced one full loop turn. What order does an agent do things in each turn?
exercises.explain: Each turn, an agent observes the current situation, reasons about what to do, then acts. That order lets it react to fresh information every cycle.

cards.title: Wait — check before you go!
cards.body: An agent just decided to run a command. But what if the command is garbled or points to a file that doesn't exist? A quick safety check can stop trouble before it starts.

cards.title: What is a heuristic safety check?
cards.body: It's a fast, simple rule that inspects a proposed action before it runs. If the action looks broken or illegal, the check blocks it early instead of letting it fail messily.

cards.title: Airport security scanner
cards.body: Before you board, your bag goes through a scanner that catches obvious problems. A safety check scans each action the same way — catching bad formats or forbidden items before takeoff.

cards.title: A check in action
cards.body: The agent proposes: delete file '###'. The check sees the filename is invalid and refuses. The agent gets told 'bad argument' and can try again — no real harm done.

cards.title: Myth: checks make the agent smart
cards.body: Not quite! A heuristic check isn't smart reasoning — it's just a quick rule that spots obvious mistakes. It catches format and legality errors, not clever ones.

cards.title: With vs. without a check
cards.body: Pre-filtering catches junk early; skipping it lets broken actions crash the system.

left.text: Bad action blocked early, agent retries safely

right.text: Broken action runs and causes a messy error

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of a heuristic safety check?

options.text: To pre-filter proposed actions and catch obvious format or illegal-argument errors before they run
To make the agent's answers longer
To delete the agent's memory after each turn
To choose which color the app uses

exercises.explain: A safety check is a quick filter placed before execution. It inspects the proposed action and blocks ones that are malformed or illegal, so they never actually run.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A heuristic safety check runs AFTER the action has already executed.
exercises.explain: False. The whole point is to check BEFORE execution. Catching a bad action afterward would be too late — the damage might already be done.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about when the check happens.
exercises.sentence: A safety check inspects an action before it can execute.
exercises.explain: The check must run 'before' execution so it can block broken or illegal actions in time, not clean up afterward.

exercises.prompt: Which reason for a safety check reads like it was written by an AI trying to sound polished but vague?

options.text: It stops a command with a missing filename from running.
It leverages proactive validation to holistically optimize action integrity across the pipeline.
It blocks an action that uses a forbidden argument.

exercises.explain: The AI-sounding one piles on buzzwords like 'holistically optimize' and 'action integrity' without saying anything concrete. The real examples name a specific error the check catches.

exercises.prompt: An agent proposes the action: move file to '<<>>'. A heuristic safety check runs first. What should happen, and why does this matter for the whole loop?

options.text: The check spots the illegal filename '<<>>', blocks the action before it runs, and returns an error so the agent can observe it and retry next turn
The check lets it run because agents should never be interrupted
The check rewrites the agent's goal to something easier
The check deletes the file to be safe

exercises.explain: A heuristic safety check pre-filters actions before they execute. Here it catches the illegal filename and blocks it, then feeds an error back into the loop. On the next observe-reason-act turn, the agent sees that error and can fix its plan — keeping the whole loop safe and self-correcting instead of crashing.
